item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image used in the article comes from here, which is non-compliant with the wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the test log, it's hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last test with 1162 must have been one hell of a discovery.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The test logs answered what-if questions in a humorous way. Made me smile. I enjoyed it. I upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the SCP, but given that the containment procedures include "activate it every so often so that it doesn't disappear on us", all the notes from the test logs on what is and isn't safe to do when activating should be included in the containment procedures, where containment staff will see them right away. The last thing the Foundation needs is a guard doing a routine activation and unexpectedly pulling out some fist-sized piece of biohazard (or worse) that disappeared on their watch a few years ago, or pulling out an innocuous knickknack but losing their work phone or flash drive full of unlisted contact numbers and access keys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And now it is!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just want to say that test log was (explicit) hilarious

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like this one, but not a fan of the word 'here' being a link. It's too late '90s GeoCities, not enough scientific document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot, mainly because it is very reminiscent of the first SCP series. It's not scary, it's not threatening unless you're unlucky and somewhat dumb, it's not showy, it's just strange. The test log is old-school, which I love. Basically, this scip calls attention back to what the SCP Foundation was originally supposed to be about: Keeping weird things that break the currently known laws of science, physics, mathematics, etc. from becoming public knowledge and creating chaos. That will always be the point of the SCP Foundation to me, especially because it allows for weird things like this that aren't trying to do anything drastic or threatening. I don't come here to be scared because OMG RANDOM THING THAT KILLS PEOPLE BECAUSE SCP IS SCARY, I come here to be entertained by creativity, by artists being inspired by the (true) premise of the SCP Foundation and writing into existence something interesting. So bravo, author; you understand the Foundation and did something awesome. I'm sorry I don't get to be the one to bring this article to +100, but I get to put it over +100, so that's good, too. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has some serious potential to go into the the SCP Containment Breach game! 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Future tests must take place while clothed, with at least one object on the person of the subject." Are telling me all the agents where naked on these tests?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As to your second complaint, there is no way to guide what a person pulls out of the hole. He lost a mag once, and that's what he happened to pull out. As to your first, shove it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
routine investigation of an area affected by SCP-184

No. Bad writer.

One (1) standard issue M1911A1 magazine, empty

And he lost/was looking for this why?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agent G: I will now attempt to pull a rabbit out of this hole. PRESTO *pulls out Keter SCP* Must have gotten the wrong hole

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the first time I've read the comments on this one of my SCPs in a long time, and I'm here to say that the SCP that created this one is referenced in the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh shit what if a Hole Dweller lived in there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for Ukelele strings

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's Clef. You really expected it NOT to be random?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something that occurs to me: what if the objects were lost in the first place because the SCP vanished them through space-time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject: D-4010
Note: D-4010 was wearing only a standard Foundation D-Class uniform, with no shoes, socks, or jewelry, and had no other items on his person during this test.
Item Retrieved: One (1) Half-full bottle of Wild Turkey bourbon
Item Lost: One (1) Foundation D-Class uniform
Notes: That was expected.

A very concise, clear, clinical description that rammed a very disturbing image into my brain that WILL NOT GO AWAY.

Think about it, how many D-class are 'Adonis-like builds', and how many are 'fat slobs like most Americans?' So even if you're prepared for it, seeing some flat slob wearing a 'prison orange' jumpsuit suddenly turn into some fat slob with his love handles, big gut and sagging glutes suddenly on display in the blink of an eye is a major shock. "That was expected." is a nice cover phrase for "Why in Cthulu's name did we do that? And why the [EXPLICATIVE REDACTED] didn't I look away when he reached into the hole?"

Wish I could upvote twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirded. Also, I think a good alternate name for this thing would be "Packrat Hole"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed.
I see a lot of bile in the comments here for the old-school style. But the old-school is the best school, IMO. Modern articles are (usually) technically better, but there just doesn't seem to be as much of that cool world-building that drew me in to the site in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, it has a taste of the old Foundation but it's there for a reason, not just because. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda like this, only because it's one of those quirky SCPs that just does random, weird shit within a set of parameters and makes for wonderful test logs.

That being said, the 1911 is a magazine-fed weapon, not clip. You've managed to tweak every gun nut who just read your article. I'd suggest using 'magazine.' Also, unless Agent K is a real old-timer, I'd suggest using 'One standard issue M9 magazine,' or at least, if you want him to be just a little bit old school salty, 'one standard issue M1911A1 magazine.'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject: Agent G█████

SUFFER LIKE G DID.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay. Normally I hate these. But I upvoted for Dr. King reaching in and pulling out a bag of 500 apple seeds. That right there is comedy gold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept doesn't hook me at all, it has an unnecessary relocation effect (I was hoping that an SCP made it, hence its location in the wall of a containment area), and it unnecessarily namedrops Dr. Clef. So yeah, not great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a lot of some of the older articles that we still have around. No vote, but interesting to see that style employed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As to the first, I'll leave that to whoever fills out the idea in the extended testing log.
As to the second, I always assumed the D-Class uniform was a jumpsuit, and I did say "with no shoes, socks, or jewelry, and had no other items on his person during this test."
As to the third, how many items have you lost during your life, even temporarily? The odds of pulling something you're looking for out and getting the right thing on the first, or second, or 500th try is astronomical, and the extended testing log shows that repeated testing can eventually get recursive; maybe it doesn't pull anything out that you're actively trying to pull out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A portion of cinderblock is currently visible through the hole; however, no light source has been able to illuminate any portion of the interior beyond that, the hole always appears to be dark.

I assume if you push a hand-held video camera through that it causes the SCP to displace?

Item Lost: One (1) Foundation D-Class uniform

Why the whole uniform, as opposed to just one piece of it? Or is the uniform a one-piece jumpsuit which includes shoes?

Agents actively searching for biological or memetic agents, or any Keter-class SCP objects, are forbidden to use SCP-1162 without a full security complement present.

That makes it way too useful: if there's any uncontained SCP which is small enough to fit through the hole, just have an agent who's looking for it draw item after item out of the hole until they get the intended SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehhhh….no vote. Basically for reasons stated above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's simple, and I like it. Feels like it fits in with a lot of the older objects. Not a huge fan of the last test log though. I see no reason to downvote this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This idea is good, but the test log is D:

(edit: looks like Kalinin said it with a bit more detail)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted because a) the concept doesn't do a whole lot for me, and b) the test logs. I'm not saying that you're a bad writer at all, because the logs are pretty much in tune with how experimentation worked for a lot of the older SCPs. But standards have evolved since then, in my opinion for the better.

I can't imagine any circumstances in which the Foundation would allow research staff to be experimental subjects for anomalous objects. That's what D-Class are for. It seems like it would be a breathtakingly negligent risk of extremely valuable Foundation assets. The notes also unpleasantly hit a "lolfoundation" note with me.

As an aside, I intensely dislike references to the older "player character" doctor characters, as they really stretch suspension of disbelief and come across frequently as name-dropping. However, that didn't factor into my downvote as that's a strictly personal preference for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is that, but it has some severe limitations. For example, it has to fit through the hole. Most Keter class SCPs are too large to fit through the hole. Also, it can be anything from the person's life that they have lost; and the extended testing log will show that there is no way to guide it. How many things have you lost in your life?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um, additional problem that hadn't occurred to me. It feels like it has too much potential. Too easy to 'find' any escaped SCP you're looking for, or one you know exists but can't find, at the cost of losing something quite mundane. In other words, for the cost of a shoelace (and possibly your hand once it materialises), you can find an escaped Keter-class SCP.

So while the last entry in the log is pretty funny, I am now considering downvoting. I'll let you work on it and see where you get to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, but lacks a real hook. No vote for now. Also, the Clef test seems a little random. It doesn't really change anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, you're right. That doesn't make sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted, but this:

Over the course of 3 years, it was watched for, activated once by any Agent who found it, and destroyed to cause an immediate shift until it reached the current containment location in Site 31.

doesn't make much sense. How does the Foundation always find it? Wouldn't it just have been easier to contain it in situ (taking the site over, that is), once they'd worked out its effect of shifting unless regularly used?
